data.data_point: Average monthly living expenses for students
data.value: 2-7 million VND/month (varies by accommodation type)
data.citation_text: Vietnam.vn reports that average monthly student living expenses range from 2-7 million VND/month, depending on accommodation type and spending habits (2024).

data.data_point: Average monthly living expenses for TP.HCM students
data.value: 5 million VND/month (average, excluding tuition)
data.citation_text: Vietnam.vn indicates that most students in Ho Chi Minh City spend an average of 5 million VND/month or more on living expenses, not including tuition fees (2024).

data.value: 9.5 million VND/year (36.3% increase from 2022)
data.citation_text: The 2024 Vietnam Household Living Standards Survey (VHLSS) conducted by GSO reports that education investment reached VND 9.5 million per student annually, representing a 36.3% increase from VND 7 million in 2022.

data.value: Urban: 10.7 million VND/student/year; Rural: 5.1 million VND/student/year
data.citation_text: GSO data (2020) shows significant urban-rural disparities in education spending: urban households spent 10.7 million VND per student annually, 2.1 times higher than rural households.

data.data_point: Part-time work participation rate among students
data.value: 22.1% of university and vocational students
data.citation_text: According to GSO data from 2018, approximately 22.1% of university and vocational students in Vietnam engage in part-time work.

data.data_point: Students working less than 5 hours per week
data.value: 30.5% (highest percentage)
data.citation_text: [uncertain] Research on part-time work patterns shows that 30.5% of surveyed university students work less than 5 hours per week, representing the highest single category of work intensity.

data.value: 20,000-60,000 VND/hour (market rate); 15,000-20,000 VND/hour (common range)
data.source_name: Multiple industry surveys and legal data
data.citation_text: Market surveys indicate part-time student wages typically range from 20,000-60,000 VND/hour, with common rates around 15,000-20,000 VND/hour, varying by job type and location (2025).

data.data_point: Minimum student hourly wage - Zone I
data.value: 25,500 VND/hour from 2026
data.geographic_scope: Zone I (TP.HCM and major urban areas)
data.citation_text: The Vietnamese Government's Decree 293/2025/NĐ-CP, effective January 1, 2026, establishes a minimum hourly wage of 25,500 VND for student workers in Zone I areas including TP.HCM.

data.value: To practice professional skills and gain experience (80.8%); To increase income (80.8%)
data.citation_text: Research on Vietnamese student part-time employment shows that 80.8% cite professional skills development and 80.8% cite income needs as motivations for working while studying.

data.value: Just sufficient: 64.7%; Difficulty: 23.4%; Never spent all allowance: 11.9%
data.citation_text: [uncertain] A student spending survey found that 64.7% report their spending level as just sufficient, 23.4% report difficulty affording expenses, and 11.9% reported never spending all their monthly allowance.

data.value: Living with family: 52.34%; Rental housing: 40.43%; Dormitory: 7.23%
data.citation_text: [uncertain] A survey of Vietnamese students shows residence patterns: 52.34% live with family, 40.43% in rental housing, and 7.23% in university dormitories.

data.value: 62% of respondents use MoMo (most popular e-wallet in Vietnam)
data.citation_text: A 2024 Decision Lab survey found that MoMo is the most widely used e-wallet in Vietnam, with 62% of respondents reporting regular usage.

data.data_point: E-wallet usage frequency among Vietnamese consumers
data.value: Weekly: 66%; 3-5 times per week: 35%; Daily: 30%
data.citation_text: Market research indicates that two-thirds of Vietnamese consumers use e-wallets on a weekly basis, with 35% using them 3-5 times per week and 30% using them daily (2024).

data.data_point: E-wallet adoption rate among Vietnamese consumers
data.value: Close to 60% of respondents; 60% of users have at least 2 e-wallet apps
data.citation_text: A 2021 survey found that e-wallet adoption reached approximately 60% among Vietnamese respondents, with over 60% of users maintaining multiple e-wallet applications.

data.value: E-banking: 37.95%; E-payments: 30.12%
data.citation_text: Research shows that young people aged under 30 in Vietnam demonstrate significantly higher fintech adoption than older demographics: 37.95% use e-banking and 30.12% use e-payments.

data.data_point: Bank transfer adoption among Vietnamese users
data.value: Most widely adopted digital payment method; Preferred for security and reliability
data.citation_text: Bank transfers are the most widely adopted digital payment method in Vietnam, with strong preference among both individuals and businesses due to perceived security and reliability.

data.data_point: Informal employment rate among young workers
data.value: 79.5% of young workers in informal employment
data.citation_text: According to ILO data from 2015, informal employment affected 79.5% of young workers in Vietnam.

data.value: 66.3% employment rate; 33.3% agriculture, 33.4% industry, 32.6% services
data.citation_text: ILO data (2015) shows that two-thirds of young Vietnamese (66.3%) were employed, with fairly even sectoral distribution across agriculture (33.3%), industry (33.4%), and services (32.6%).

data.value: 18.3% of young adolescents aged 15-17 out of school and working
data.citation_text: ILO statistics indicate that 18.3% of young adolescents aged 15-17 in Vietnam were out of school and engaged in work (2015).

data.value: 34.6% in vulnerable employment: 12.9% own-account workers, 21.7% unpaid family workers
data.citation_text: ILO data (2015) reveals that more than one-third (34.6%) of young workers in Vietnam remain in vulnerable employment, including own-account workers (12.9%) and unpaid family workers (21.7%).

data.data_point: Student average monthly part-time work income
data.value: 1-2 million VND/month (30 hours/week); Factory workers: 250,000-400,000 VND/shift
data.citation_text: [uncertain] Research on Vietnamese student part-time earnings shows typical monthly income ranges from 1-2 million VND from approximately 30 hours/week, with factory workers earning 250,000-400,000 VND per shift.

data.value: Overall score: 66.8 points (ages 20-29); 68.5 points (ages 30-39); Financial situation: 55.7 points; Long-term planning: 23.8 points
data.citation_text: [uncertain] Financial literacy assessments show Gen Z in Vietnam scores 66.8 points overall (ages 20-29), with weak long-term financial planning scores of 23.8 points (2024).

data.value: Only 54.8% of young people avoid overdrafts
data.citation_text: [uncertain] Financial behavior research shows that only 54.8% of young Vietnamese avoid overdrafts, indicating concerning spending patterns among this demographic (2024).

data.data_point: Young adults spending more than monthly income
data.value: 52.2-47.8% spend more than their monthly income allows
data.citation_text: [uncertain] A financial behavior survey found that nearly half (52.2-47.8%) of young Vietnamese spend more than their monthly income allows (2024).

data.value: 72.8% have emergency savings
data.citation_text: [uncertain] Survey data indicates that 72.8% of young Vietnamese maintain emergency savings funds (2024).

data.value: 70.6% maintain a budget
data.citation_text: [uncertain] Financial management surveys show that 70.6% of young Vietnamese maintain a personal budget (2024).

data.data_point: Vietnam NEET youth (Not in Employment, Education, or Training)
data.value: 1.4 million (10.2% of youth population, ages 15-24)
data.citation_text: GSO data from Q4 2025 reports that Vietnam had approximately 1.4 million young people aged 15-24 not engaged in employment, education, or training, representing 10.2% of the total youth population.

data.data_point: Facebook as job search platform for students
data.value: 38% of Vietnamese university students find part-time jobs via Facebook
data.citation_text: Market research from Q&Me shows that 38% of Vietnamese university students rely on Facebook to find part-time employment opportunities (2023).

data.data_point: Online transaction value via mobile wallets
data.value: USD 18.2 billion in 2024; 73% cleared via mobile wallet super-apps
data.citation_text: Analysis of Vietnam's mobile payments sector shows that online transaction values exceeded USD 18.2 billion in 2024, with 73% processed through mobile wallet super-app integrations (2024).

data.value: Over 31 million users with 140,000 payment points
data.citation_text: MoMo has expanded to over 31 million users with 140,000 merchant payment points, processing 5.5 billion transactions in Q1 2025 and achieving first-time profitability in 2024.

data.value: Nearly 80% of young adults in top quintile have attended university
data.citation_text: [uncertain] World Bank analysis (2020) shows significant equity gaps: nearly 80% of young adults in the top income quintile attend university, while bottom two quintiles account for only 10% of enrollments.

data.data_point: Tuition fees as share of education costs
data.value: Public sector: 46% of direct cost; Private sector: 69% of direct cost
data.citation_text: World Bank data (2016) indicates that tuition fees comprise 46% of direct education costs in public institutions and 69% in private institutions, reflecting significant student cost-sharing.
